content: Rajaraman argues that 'meh' is not a failure state but the natural equilibrium of consensus-driven product development. Most products end up functionally adequate and emotionally neutral because no one on the team hated them enough to block shipping — and that absence of objection is mistaken for quality. The enemy of great products is not bad ideas but inoffensive ones.
implication: Product leaders should treat the absence of strong negative internal reactions as a warning sign, not a green light. If everyone on the team is comfortable with the product, it is probably not interesting enough to make anyone outside the team care deeply.
source_ref: Product Management: Going from Meh to Awesome by Shiva Rajaraman at Mind the Product 2015

content: The gap between a product that is tolerated and one that is loved is not a feature gap — it is a feeling gap. Rajaraman contends that adding more capabilities to a mediocre product rarely transforms it into something users love, because the deficit is emotional rather than functional. The path from meh to awesome requires identifying and amplifying the specific moments that generate genuine feeling, not appending more utility.
implication: Before prioritizing the next feature, product teams should audit which existing moments in the product make users feel something, and ask whether investment is going toward protecting and deepening those moments or diluting them with more surface area.
source_ref: Product Management: Going from Meh to Awesome by Shiva Rajaraman at Mind the Product 2015

content: Rajaraman frames the meh diagnosis as a discipline of precision rather than a general judgment. 'Meh' is not a cloud that hangs over a whole product — it is a collection of specific, locatable moments where the experience is functional but inert. The practice of identifying each of those moments individually, naming what is missing, and addressing them one by one is the core craft of elevating product quality.
implication: Teams should resist the tendency to describe product quality problems in aggregate and instead map the specific moments — flows, transitions, empty states, error conditions — where the experience goes flat. Each one is a discrete opportunity, not a symptom of an unfixable whole.
source_ref: Product Management: Going from Meh to Awesome by Shiva Rajaraman at Mind the Product 2015

content: Rajaraman argues that measuring product quality too early — before the core magic has been established — produces a particularly insidious form of mediocrity. When teams instrument and optimize before identifying the emotional kernel of the product, they end up improving tolerance metrics rather than love metrics, and users who score 4/5 on satisfaction are not the same as users who would be devastated if the product disappeared. Optimizing the wrong thing with rigor is worse than not optimizing at all.
implication: Before defining your North Star metric, define your North Star feeling — the specific emotional state the product should reliably produce. Only then does instrumentation become a tool for protecting and deepening that feeling rather than a mechanism for smoothing it into adequacy.
source_ref: DDD Episode 008: Managing Products with Data ft. Shiva Rajaraman

content: Rajaraman observes that the products which define eras are almost always the products of a strong, sometimes controversial individual point of view rather than a committee-averaged one. Consensus processes are structurally designed to eliminate the things some people dislike, which is precisely what makes them eliminate the things that would make other people love the product. The products most people use but feel neutral about are usually the ones built by committees.
implication: Great product organizations need structural mechanisms that protect a singular creative point of view from the smoothing effects of cross-functional consensus — not by ignoring input, but by ensuring that conviction survives the review process and that someone is empowered to hold the vision accountable to quality rather than to alignment.
source_ref: Product Management: Going from Meh to Awesome by Shiva Rajaraman at Mind the Product 2015

content: Rajaraman treats taste as a strategic organizational asset rather than a soft personal quality. Organizations that cultivate refined aesthetic and emotional judgment attract people who share it, who make products that reflect it, which in turn attract users who value it — creating a self-reinforcing flywheel. Deferring investment in taste until after product-market fit is found means building a deficit that becomes harder to reverse the longer it compounds.
implication: Taste is not something to hire for later once the product has proven itself — it is an input to product-market fit, not a reward for achieving it. Leaders should assess their organization's current taste level and treat any deficit as a strategic risk comparable to a technical debt problem.
source_ref: Product Management: Going from Meh to Awesome by Shiva Rajaraman at Mind the Product 2015

content: Rajaraman argues that the qualitative magic of a product — the ineffable quality that makes users feel something they cannot fully articulate — cannot be A/B tested into existence. It must be discovered, recognized, and protected before the optimization machine is turned on. Product organizations that invert this sequence end up running experiments on a foundation that was never magical to begin with.
implication: The sequencing question matters: has the team identified and validated the core emotional magic before beginning optimization? If not, every experiment is potentially an experiment in the wrong direction — making the tolerable more efficient rather than making the magical more accessible.
source_ref: Product Management: Going from Meh to Awesome by Shiva Rajaraman at Mind the Product 2015

content: One of Rajaraman's diagnostic tools for product quality is the 'would they be devastated' test — the question of whether users would genuinely feel a loss if the product disappeared tomorrow. He draws a sharp distinction between users who would be mildly inconvenienced and users who would feel a real absence, arguing that only the latter group signals that the product has crossed the threshold from meh to awesome. Usage volume is a poor proxy for this distinction.
implication: Supplement engagement metrics with structured qualitative research asking users to describe how they would feel if the product no longer existed. The intensity and emotional specificity of their answers is a more honest quality signal than session length or DAU.
source_ref: Product Management: Going from Meh to Awesome by Shiva Rajaraman at Mind the Product 2015

content: Rajaraman points to emergent user behaviors — the ways users repurpose, hack, or extend a product beyond its intended design — as the clearest evidence that a product has crossed into territory users genuinely love. These behaviors are not edge cases to be managed; they are signals that the product has touched something real and that users care enough to invest creative energy in it. The right response is to study and amplify these behaviors, not suppress them.
implication: Product teams should actively monitor for use cases they did not design for and treat high-effort emergent behaviors as a direct indicator of emotional investment. Those behaviors often point toward the next dimension of genuine product love.
source_ref: Product Management: Going from Meh to Awesome by Shiva Rajaraman at Mind the Product 2015

content: Rajaraman observes that scale, when applied to a product that has not yet achieved genuine magic, amplifies mediocrity rather than enabling improvement. A product used by millions who feel nothing about it is a worse outcome than one used by thousands who would fight to keep it. The pressure to grow before the core emotional quality is established is one of the most common ways promising products are destroyed by their own growth.
implication: Growth strategy should be gated by quality milestones, not just adoption milestones. Before scaling distribution, teams should be able to point to a cohort of users who are genuinely devoted — not just retained — and understand precisely what is producing that devotion.
source_ref: Finding product-market fit & driving growth with Shiva Rajaraman / Keynote & Live Q&A

content: Rajaraman argues that when a product experience has persistent friction at a particular seam, the cause is almost always an organizational boundary or incentive misalignment rather than a design failure. The shape of the organization casts a shadow on the shape of the product, and the most intractable product quality problems are often invisible until the team realizes they are actually organizational design problems in disguise.
implication: When a product quality problem resists repeated design and engineering fixes, leaders should look at the organizational structure around that problem — who owns it, whose metrics are implicated, and where the boundaries of accountability sit. The fix is often structural before it is technical.
source_ref: Product Management: Going from Meh to Awesome by Shiva Rajaraman at Mind the Product 2015

content: Rajaraman's experience across YouTube, Spotify, and OpenSea led him to develop a consistent view that data reveals what users are experiencing but cannot substitute for thinking about what they actually need. He distinguishes between metrics that surface user experience — which are genuinely valuable — and metrics that get optimized at the expense of user experience, which produce precisely the kind of functional-but-loveless product he diagnoses as meh. Asking 'what is this number hiding?' is as important as asking what it shows.
implication: Healthy data culture requires teams to regularly interrogate their own metrics — asking not just what is improving, but what might be being suppressed or obscured by optimizing for a given number. Metrics that look good on dashboards can be the precise mechanism by which genuine quality is traded away.
source_ref: DDD Episode 008: Managing Products with Data ft. Shiva Rajaraman

content: Rajaraman frames the transition from meh to awesome as a shift from measuring tolerance to cultivating love. Tolerance metrics — things like low churn, acceptable task completion, and acceptable satisfaction scores — tell you that users have accommodated themselves to the product. Love metrics — devoted cohorts, voluntary advocacy, genuine devastation at potential loss — tell you the product has actually crossed into something worth building on. These are fundamentally different signals and require different organizational responses.
implication: Teams should explicitly distinguish between their tolerance metrics and their love metrics, and ensure leadership attention and investment are proportionally directed toward the latter. A product optimized purely for tolerance metrics will never make the meh-to-awesome leap.
source_ref: Product Management: Going from Meh to Awesome by Shiva Rajaraman at Mind the Product 2015

content: Rajaraman argues that the 'meh to awesome' discipline is not a one-time product transformation but an ongoing organizational practice. Products regress toward meh as they grow — as more stakeholders add their requirements, as safety nets of metrics accumulate, as the original creative conviction gets distributed across a larger team. Sustaining awesomeness requires active, repeated effort to protect the emotional core of the product against the entropy of organizational growth.
implication: Product leaders at scale should periodically audit whether the product's core emotional magic is still intact — and treat any erosion of that magic as an urgent quality regression, not an acceptable tradeoff for breadth or safety. The maintenance of awesomeness is as demanding as its initial achievement.
source_ref: Product Management: Going from Meh to Awesome by Shiva Rajaraman at Mind the Product 2015

content: Rajaraman's cross-platform career led him to recognize that the specific sources of meh differ by product category and emotional stakes. Audio products sit at an unusually high point on the intimacy gradient because they accompany users through the most private moments of their lives — which means that personalization failures in audio feel more like personal betrayals than technical errors. The higher the emotional intimacy of a product, the more consequential each meh moment becomes.
implication: Product teams should calibrate the urgency of meh-elimination to the emotional stakes of their category. In high-intimacy products — audio, health, communications — each neutral or off-putting moment carries more relationship damage than it would in a lower-stakes utility product.
source_ref: 5 Q's for Shiva Rajaraman, Vice President of Product at Spotify

content: Rajaraman argues that the most dangerous form of product mediocrity is the kind that ships because no one felt strongly enough to block it. Strong conviction held by an individual — even when others are uncertain — is the necessary precondition for building products that genuinely surprise and delight. The organizational challenge is not to generate consensus around great ideas but to protect great ideas from the friction of consensus-seeking.
implication: Product processes should include explicit mechanisms for protecting individual conviction — stage gates where a single empowered person can say 'this is not good enough yet' and have that judgment respected without requiring justification by committee. The veto for quality is as important as the veto for safety.
source_ref: Product Management: Going from Meh to Awesome by Shiva Rajaraman at Mind the Product 2015

content: Rajaraman uses his experience at WeWork to extend the meh-to-awesome framework beyond software into physical environments. He argues that physical spaces have the same emotional quality spectrum as digital products — they can be functional and forgettable or genuinely inspiring — and that digital-native product thinking (instrumented feedback, rapid iteration, user-centered design) can be applied to space design just as directly as to software. The medium is different; the quality discipline is the same.
implication: Teams designing physical environments — offices, retail, events, hospitality — should borrow the diagnostic vocabulary of product quality: mapping the specific moments where the experience goes flat, instrumenting user behavior where possible, and iterating toward delight rather than accepting functional adequacy as sufficient.
source_ref: How WeWork Creates an On-Demand Workspace

content: Rajaraman's approach to the meh-to-awesome transition always begins backwards from the human moment rather than forward from the technical capability. He asks what a specific person needs to feel at a specific point in their experience before selecting the mechanism that will produce that feeling. This sequencing — emotion first, capability second — is what prevents products from accumulating technically impressive features that fail to add up to a feeling of coherence or love.
implication: Product briefs and design sprints should lead with the desired emotional state — written in the vocabulary of human experience, not feature descriptions — and treat technology selection as a downstream question. Teams that lead with capability tend to build impressive products that feel cold; teams that lead with the human moment tend to build simpler products that feel right.
source_ref: Product Management: Going from Meh to Awesome by Shiva Rajaraman at Mind the Product 2015

content: Rajaraman notes that the 'meh to awesome' threshold is not a gradient but a discontinuity — products do not slide smoothly from adequate to loved, they cross a threshold. Below the threshold, additional improvements compound into continued adequacy; above it, even small improvements amplify a sense of love. The challenge is identifying what the threshold crossing requires, because it is almost never the next obvious feature on the roadmap.
implication: Teams should resist the temptation to treat product quality as a linear improvement problem where more work = more love. Identifying what the threshold crossing specifically requires — which is usually an emotional, not a functional insight — is worth more investment than incrementally improving everything.
source_ref: Product Management: Going from Meh to Awesome by Shiva Rajaraman at Mind the Product 2015

content: Rajaraman argues that a product team that cannot override its own data is not empowered — it is imprisoned. The discipline of data-driven product management includes knowing when a metric is wrong, when it is measuring the wrong thing, or when the right call is to move in a direction the current data does not yet support. Teams that have abdicated this judgment in favor of full data deference have outsourced their conviction to their dashboards — and conviction is what separates meh from awesome.
implication: Data-driven does not mean data-determined. Product leaders should make explicit the conditions under which they will override their metrics, document those decisions and their rationale, and treat the willingness to make conviction-based calls as a sign of organizational maturity rather than a failure of rigor.
source_ref: DDD Episode 008: Managing Products with Data ft. Shiva Rajaraman

content: Rajaraman draws a sharp distinction between products users accept and products users seek. Meh products are accepted — users choose them when presented, tolerate their limitations, and would mildly prefer an alternative if one were offered. Awesome products are sought — users actively return to them, recommend them unprompted, and feel a proprietary attachment to them. The behavioral difference between these two states is significant, but the emotional difference is even larger, and it is the emotional difference that drives long-term retention and advocacy.
implication: User acquisition and retention metrics do not distinguish between accepted and sought products — both can look similar in a dashboard. Teams should design qualitative research specifically to answer the question: are users choosing us, or are we just the path of least resistance? The answer determines how durable the business is.
source_ref: Product Management: Going from Meh to Awesome by Shiva Rajaraman at Mind the Product 2015

content: Rajaraman's meh-to-awesome framework is explicitly a framework for subtraction as much as addition. Identifying and eliminating the specific moments that produce neutrality — dead ends, uninspired copy, forgettable transitions, unnecessary friction — is as much a part of the quality leap as investing in moments of delight. Products that never subtract are always accumulating meh faster than they are generating awesome.
implication: Roadmap planning should include a regular subtractive audit — a deliberate inventory of what to cut, simplify, or redesign because it currently produces emotional neutrality. This is as strategically important as the feature roadmap, and should receive proportional executive attention.
source_ref: Product Management: Going from Meh to Awesome by Shiva Rajaraman at Mind the Product 2015

content: Rajaraman sees the application of AI as one of the most powerful tools currently available for making the meh-to-awesome leap — not because AI features impress users, but because AI done well makes experiences feel effortlessly right in a way users cannot fully explain. He calls this 'everyday magic': AI that disappears into the product and makes capabilities feel inevitable rather than impressive. The moment a user has to think about the AI, the magic has already partially failed.
implication: Teams building AI-powered products should evaluate success not by whether users notice the AI, but by whether the overall experience feels more effortlessly right than before. The goal is not visible intelligence — it is invisible appropriateness. If users are impressed by the AI, that is a secondary outcome; if they are delighted by the experience, that is the primary one.
source_ref: Everyday Magic: How AI Tools Are Changing Consumer Creativity

content: Rajaraman's cross-platform career — spanning YouTube, Spotify, WeWork, Facebook, and OpenSea — taught him that the meh-to-awesome frameworks he developed in one context are best treated as hypotheses when imported to another. The emotional quality that makes audio awesome is different from what makes a marketplace awesome, which is different from what makes a physical space awesome. The transferable insight is the discipline and the diagnostic instinct, not the specific answer from the prior domain.
implication: Leaders transitioning between product domains should import their quality diagnostic instincts without importing their domain-specific answers. The first question in a new context should always be: what does 'awesome' feel like to the specific user in this specific moment — not 'what worked in my last job?'
source_ref: In Conversation with Shiva Rajaraman, VP of Product, OpenSea
